Front: What is a database index and how does it actually improve query performance?
Back: A database index is a separate data structure (often a B-tree) that maps column values to row locations so the engine can jump directly to matching rows instead of scanning the entire table.
Why: Indexes matter because most query cost comes from disk I/O, and avoiding full scans keeps performance stable as data grows.
When: Use indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY, or GROUP BY with good selectivity.
Tradeoffs: Indexes speed reads but slow writes since every insert/update must maintain the index, and they consume extra storage.
Trap: Mistake: adding indexes to every column. Why wrong: low-selectivity indexes are often ignored. Consequence: wasted space and slower writes with no read benefit.
Scenario: A login query uses an email index to avoid scanning millions of users.

Front: Why does index selectivity matter when designing indexes?
Back: Selectivity measures how unique values are; high selectivity lets the engine narrow results quickly, while low selectivity still touches many rows so the index provides little benefit.
Why: It matters because the optimizer chooses plans based on how much data must be read, and low selectivity often leads to full scans.
When: Use indexes on high-cardinality columns like IDs or emails, not boolean flags.
Tradeoffs: High selectivity improves read performance, but indexing many columns increases write overhead and storage cost.
Trap: Mistake: indexing a boolean column. Why wrong: large portions of the table still match. Consequence: optimizer skips the index and you still pay maintenance cost.
Scenario: Filtering on is_active often scans most rows despite having an index.

Front: What is a composite index and how does column order affect it?
Back: A composite index stores multiple columns in a defined order, and the engine can efficiently use only the leftmost prefix of that order.
Why: Order matters because queries must match the leading columns to benefit from the index.
When: Use composite indexes when queries filter on multiple columns together, placing the most selective or common filter first.
Tradeoffs: They speed multi-column queries but increase size and write cost, and wrong ordering reduces usefulness.
Trap: Mistake: putting a low-selectivity column first. Why wrong: it doesn’t narrow results early. Consequence: large index scans or optimizer skipping it.
Scenario: Indexing (status, user_id) is worse than (user_id, status) when user_id is more selective.

Front: What is a covering index and why can it eliminate table lookups?
Back: A covering index includes all columns needed for a query, so the engine can answer it directly from the index without reading the base table.
Why: This matters because avoiding table lookups removes extra I/O, which is often the dominant cost.
When: Use for frequently executed queries with predictable column access.
Tradeoffs: They improve read speed but increase index size and memory usage and slow writes.
Trap: Mistake: adding too many columns to cover everything. Why wrong: bloated indexes hurt cache efficiency. Consequence: overall performance degrades.
Scenario: An index on (email, id) serves a query without touching the table.

Front: What is index fragmentation and how does it affect performance?
Back: Fragmentation occurs when index pages become disordered due to inserts and updates, increasing page reads and reducing locality.
Why: It matters because fragmented indexes require more I/O to traverse, slowing queries over time.
When: Monitor and rebuild or reorganize indexes in write-heavy systems.
Tradeoffs: Rebuilding restores performance but consumes resources and may lock tables.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring fragmentation. Why wrong: degradation is gradual and unnoticed. Consequence: queries get slower over time.
Scenario: A busy orders table slows until indexes are rebuilt.

Front: Why can too many indexes degrade overall system performance?
Back: Every write must update all relevant indexes, so having many indexes increases insert, update, and delete cost.
Why: It matters because write-heavy systems can become bottlenecked by index maintenance.
When: Limit indexes to those supporting real query patterns.
Tradeoffs: More indexes improve reads but degrade writes and increase storage.
Trap: Mistake: indexing every column proactively. Why wrong: many indexes are never used. Consequence: slower writes and wasted space.
Scenario: A logging system slows due to maintaining unused indexes.

Front: What is a partial index and when should you use it?
Back: A partial index only includes rows matching a condition, reducing size and focusing on relevant data.
Why: It matters because smaller indexes are faster and more cache-friendly for targeted queries.
When: Use when queries consistently filter on a subset like status='active'.
Tradeoffs: They are efficient for specific queries but useless outside the condition.
Trap: Mistake: expecting it to work for all queries. Why wrong: it only covers filtered rows. Consequence: missed optimization.
Scenario: Indexing only active users improves targeted queries.

Front: What is the difference between clustered and non-clustered indexes?
Back: A clustered index defines the physical order of rows, while a non-clustered index stores separate pointers to rows.
Why: It matters because physical ordering improves range queries and data locality.
When: Use clustered indexes for primary keys or range-based queries.
Tradeoffs: Only one clustered index exists, and changing it is expensive.
Trap: Mistake: picking a poor clustered key. Why wrong: affects entire table layout. Consequence: inefficient access patterns.
Scenario: Clustering by timestamp speeds time-range queries.

Front: Why are range queries particularly sensitive to index design?
Back: Range queries traverse ordered index segments, so proper indexing enables efficient sequential access instead of scanning the table.
Why: It matters because large ranges still read many rows, so design affects how much data is touched.
When: Use indexes on columns used in BETWEEN, >, or < filters.
Tradeoffs: Indexes help but large ranges still require scanning many entries.
Trap: Mistake: assuming index makes all ranges fast. Why wrong: wide ranges still scan heavily. Consequence: high I/O.
Scenario: Querying a year of orders still scans many index entries.

Front: What is a hash index and when is it useful?
Back: A hash index maps keys via a hash function for fast equality lookups but has no ordering for range queries.
Why: It matters because it excels at exact matches but cannot support sorting or ranges.
Tradeoffs: Fast for equality but unusable for ordered queries.
Trap: Mistake: using it for range queries. Why wrong: no order. Consequence: fallback to full scan.
Scenario: User ID lookup is fast, but ID range query is not.

Front: What role does the query optimizer play in index usage?
Back: The optimizer evaluates execution plans and chooses whether an index or full scan is cheaper based on statistics and cost estimates.
Why: It matters because indexes only help if the optimizer decides to use them.
When: Keep statistics updated to guide decisions.
Tradeoffs: Optimizers improve performance but can misestimate with stale stats.
Trap: Mistake: assuming index is always used. Why wrong: optimizer may choose scan. Consequence: unexpected slow queries.
Scenario: A query ignores an index due to outdated stats.

Front: Why do indexes help JOIN performance?
Back: Indexes on join keys allow quick lookups between tables instead of scanning entire datasets.
Why: It matters because joins can become extremely expensive at scale without indexes.
When: Index foreign keys and join columns.
Tradeoffs: They speed joins but increase write cost.
Trap: Mistake: joining without indexes. Why wrong: leads to nested scans. Consequence: exponential slowdown.
Scenario: Joining large tables without indexes causes major delays.

Front: What is index-only scan and why is it faster?
Back: An index-only scan retrieves all needed data from the index without accessing the base table.
Why: It matters because avoiding table access reduces I/O significantly.
When: Use covering indexes to enable it.
Tradeoffs: Requires extra index storage and maintenance.
Trap: Mistake: assuming all indexes support it. Why wrong: missing columns force table access. Consequence: slower queries.
Scenario: A query reads only indexed columns and skips table access.

Front: How do writes impact index maintenance internally?
Back: Each write updates index structures in addition to the table, requiring inserts or modifications within index trees.
Why: It matters because write cost grows with the number of indexes.
Tradeoffs: More indexes increase write latency.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring write cost. Why wrong: leads to slow ingestion. Consequence: throughput bottlenecks.
Scenario: High-ingest systems slow due to index updates.

Front: Why can an index become a bottleneck under heavy writes?
Back: Frequent writes can create contention on index pages, especially with sequential keys or hotspots.
Why: It matters because contention limits scalability under load.
When: Design indexes to distribute writes evenly.
Tradeoffs: Indexes improve reads but may limit write throughput.
Trap: Mistake: using sequential keys without care. Why wrong: creates hotspots. Consequence: contention and slowdown.
Scenario: Auto-increment IDs can cause index page contention.

Front: What is the leftmost prefix rule in composite indexes?
Back: A composite index can only be efficiently used starting from its first column and proceeding left to right.
Why: It matters because skipping the first column makes the index unusable.
Tradeoffs: Improves performance but reduces flexibility.
Trap: Mistake: filtering on second column only. Why wrong: index cannot be used. Consequence: full scan.
Scenario: Index (A,B) won’t help filtering only on B.

Front: Why do indexes not always improve performance?
Back: Indexes may be ignored if a query returns a large portion of the table, making a scan cheaper.
Why: It matters because indexes are not universally beneficial.
Tradeoffs: They help reads but can hurt bulk operations.
Trap: Mistake: assuming indexes always help. Why wrong: optimizer may choose scan. Consequence: wasted resources.
Scenario: A query returning most rows uses a full scan.

Front: How does index size affect performance?
Back: Larger indexes reduce cache efficiency and increase I/O because fewer entries fit in memory.
Why: It matters because memory locality strongly impacts performance.
Tradeoffs: More columns increase usefulness but reduce efficiency.
Trap: Mistake: overloading indexes. Why wrong: hurts caching. Consequence: slower queries.
Scenario: A bloated index performs worse than a lean one.

Front: What is index rebuild vs reorganization?
Back: Rebuild recreates the index completely, while reorganization defragments it incrementally.
Why: It matters because each has different cost and impact.
When: Use rebuild for heavy fragmentation, reorg for light.
Tradeoffs: Rebuild is thorough but resource-heavy.
Trap: Mistake: always rebuilding. Why wrong: unnecessary cost. Consequence: wasted resources.
Scenario: Light fragmentation doesn’t require rebuild.

Front: What is a unique index and how does it enforce constraints?
Back: A unique index ensures no duplicate values exist, enforcing data integrity at the database level.
Why: It matters because it prevents invalid data even under concurrency.
Tradeoffs: Adds integrity but slightly increases write cost.
Trap: Mistake: relying only on app validation. Why wrong: race conditions. Consequence: duplicate data.
Scenario: Concurrent inserts create duplicates without a unique index.

Front: What is index cardinality and how does it influence query planning?
Back: Cardinality measures distinct values in a column; higher cardinality improves selectivity and index usefulness.
Why: It matters because it influences optimizer decisions.
Tradeoffs: High cardinality improves reads but increases write overhead.
Trap: Mistake: confusing row count with cardinality. Why wrong: distinct values matter. Consequence: poor index choices.
Scenario: User IDs have high cardinality and are ideal for indexing.

Front: Why can an index be ignored even when it exists?
Back: The optimizer may skip an index if it estimates a scan is cheaper due to low selectivity or stale statistics.
Why: It matters because index presence does not guarantee usage.
When: Keep stats updated and queries selective.
Tradeoffs: Indexes add overhead but may not be used.
Trap: Mistake: assuming index guarantees performance. Why wrong: optimizer decides. Consequence: unexpected slowness.
Scenario: Query returns most rows so index is skipped.

Front: What is index scan vs index seek?
Back: An index seek directly locates rows, while an index scan reads large portions of the index sequentially.
Why: It matters because seeks are far more efficient for selective queries.
Tradeoffs: Seeks are fast but require selective filters.
Trap: Mistake: assuming all index usage is fast. Why wrong: scans can be expensive. Consequence: poor performance.
Scenario: Filtering by ID uses seek; broad filter uses scan.

Front: Why does updating indexed columns cost more than non-indexed ones?
Back: Updating indexed columns requires modifying both table and index structures.
Why: It matters because write-heavy workloads are sensitive to this overhead.
Tradeoffs: Indexes improve reads but degrade writes.
Trap: Mistake: indexing frequently changing fields. Why wrong: high update cost. Consequence: slower writes.
Scenario: Updating status column slows due to index maintenance.

Front: What is a bitmap index and when is it useful?
Back: A bitmap index uses bitmaps for values, making it efficient for low-cardinality columns in read-heavy systems.
Why: It matters because it can compress and accelerate analytical queries.
Tradeoffs: Efficient for reads but expensive to update.
Trap: Mistake: using in OLTP systems. Why wrong: high update cost. Consequence: poor performance.
Scenario: Categorical analytics queries benefit from bitmap indexes.

Front: How does index ordering help ORDER BY queries?
Back: Indexes maintain sorted order, allowing queries to return ordered results without extra sorting.
Why: It matters because sorting large datasets is expensive.
When: Use indexes aligned with ORDER BY.
Tradeoffs: Improves reads but adds maintenance cost.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring index order. Why wrong: requires sort. Consequence: slow queries.
Scenario: Index on created_at speeds sorted queries.

Front: What is index intersection?
Back: Index intersection combines multiple indexes to satisfy a query when no single index covers all conditions.
Why: It matters because it provides flexibility but is less efficient than a composite index.
When: Use when composite index is unavailable.
Tradeoffs: Flexible but slower than dedicated index.
Trap: Mistake: relying on intersection instead of composite index. Why wrong: less efficient. Consequence: slower queries.
Scenario: Query uses two indexes instead of one optimized composite index.

Front: Why do indexes improve JOIN performance more than filtering sometimes?
Back: Indexes on join keys enable fast lookups across tables, avoiding full scans during joins.
Why: It matters because joins can dominate query cost.
Tradeoffs: Improves joins but increases write cost.
Trap: Mistake: indexing only one table. Why wrong: other side still scans. Consequence: slow joins.
Scenario: Joining without index causes full scans.

Front: What is index clustering factor?
Back: Clustering factor measures how closely index order matches table storage order, affecting I/O efficiency.
Why: It matters because poor clustering increases random I/O.
Tradeoffs: Good clustering improves reads but may degrade over time.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring clustering. Why wrong: impacts performance. Consequence: slower queries.
Scenario: Poor clustering causes excessive disk reads.

Front: Why are covering indexes especially useful in high-throughput systems?
Back: Covering indexes eliminate table lookups, reducing I/O and improving throughput for hot queries.
Why: It matters because high-throughput systems are often I/O-bound.
Tradeoffs: Improves speed but increases index size.
Trap: Mistake: overusing covering indexes. Why wrong: bloats indexes. Consequence: memory pressure.
Scenario: Hot queries run entirely from index.

Front: What is index skew and how does it affect performance?
Back: Index skew occurs when certain values dominate a column, reducing selectivity so the index cannot efficiently narrow results.
Why: It matters because skewed data leads the optimizer to misestimate costs and choose inefficient plans.
When: Analyze real data distribution before indexing.
Tradeoffs: Balanced distribution improves performance, but real-world data is often skewed.
Trap: Mistake: assuming uniform distribution. Why wrong: skew reduces selectivity. Consequence: index scans or poor plans.
Scenario: A popular category dominates queries, making the index ineffective.

Front: What is the impact of NULL values on indexes?
Back: Indexes often exclude or poorly handle NULL-heavy columns, so they provide little filtering benefit when most values are NULL.
Why: It matters because low selectivity leads the optimizer to ignore the index.
When: Index nullable columns only when queries target non-null subsets.
Tradeoffs: Indexes can help targeted queries but waste space when mostly NULL.
Trap: Mistake: indexing a mostly NULL column. Why wrong: low usefulness. Consequence: wasted index and no performance gain.
Scenario: A column with mostly NULL values rarely benefits from indexing.

Front: Why do large IN clauses affect index usage?
Back: Large IN clauses increase lookup cost and can cause the optimizer to switch from index seeks to scans.
Why: It matters because query structure can override index benefits.
When: Use temp tables or joins for large value sets.
Tradeoffs: Indexes help small sets but degrade with large lists.
Trap: Mistake: assuming IN always uses indexes. Why wrong: optimizer may switch strategy. Consequence: slower queries.
Scenario: Thousands of IDs in an IN clause degrade performance.

Front: What is a filtered index vs partial index?
Back: Both index only rows matching a condition, reducing size and improving performance for targeted queries, though naming differs by database.
Why: It matters because smaller indexes are faster and more cache-efficient.
When: Use for frequently filtered subsets like active records.
Tradeoffs: Improves targeted queries but is not universally supported.
Trap: Mistake: assuming portability. Why wrong: syntax differs by DB. Consequence: migration issues.
Scenario: SQL Server filtered index vs PostgreSQL partial index.

Front: How do statistics affect index usage?
Back: Statistics describe data distribution so the optimizer can estimate cost and decide whether an index is beneficial.
Why: It matters because stale stats lead to incorrect plans.
When: Update stats regularly on changing data.
Tradeoffs: Accurate stats improve plans but add maintenance overhead.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring statistics. Why wrong: optimizer guesses poorly. Consequence: inefficient queries.
Scenario: Outdated stats cause index to be skipped.

Front: Why do indexes not help with functions in WHERE clauses?
Back: Functions on indexed columns break sargability, preventing the optimizer from using index ordering for efficient lookups.
Why: It matters because it forces full scans instead of seeks.
When: Rewrite queries to use raw column values.
Tradeoffs: Functions add flexibility but disable index usage.
Trap: Mistake: wrapping indexed column in a function. Why wrong: index cannot be used. Consequence: full table scan.
Scenario: LOWER(email) prevents index usage.

Front: What is index hinting and when should it be avoided?
Back: Index hints force the optimizer to use a specific index, bypassing cost-based decisions.
Why: It matters because hints can become wrong as data evolves.
When: Use only for known, stable edge cases.
Tradeoffs: Can fix issues but makes queries brittle.
Trap: Mistake: overusing hints. Why wrong: optimizer is bypassed. Consequence: long-term performance degradation.
Scenario: A forced index becomes inefficient as data grows.

Front: Why does index maintenance become critical at scale?
Back: As data grows, fragmentation and page splits increase, degrading index efficiency and query performance.
Why: It matters because small inefficiencies scale into major slowdowns.
When: Schedule rebuilds or reorganizations for large datasets.
Tradeoffs: Maintenance improves performance but consumes resources.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring maintenance. Why wrong: fragmentation accumulates. Consequence: slow queries.
Scenario: Large DB slows without index maintenance.

Front: What is index compression and when is it useful?
Back: Index compression reduces storage size, improving cache efficiency and reducing I/O at the cost of CPU during access.
Why: It matters because memory locality strongly impacts performance.
Tradeoffs: Saves space but increases CPU overhead.
Trap: Mistake: over-compressing. Why wrong: CPU cost outweighs benefit. Consequence: slower queries.
Scenario: Compressed index improves storage but adds CPU cost.

Front: Why do write-heavy systems require different indexing strategies?
Back: Write-heavy systems must minimize indexes because each write updates all indexes, increasing latency and contention.
Why: It matters because indexing overhead directly limits throughput.
When: Use only essential indexes in write-heavy systems.
Tradeoffs: Improves writes but may degrade read performance.
Trap: Mistake: applying read-heavy indexing. Why wrong: increases write cost. Consequence: bottlenecks.
Scenario: Logging system avoids excessive indexing.

Front: How do database transactions ensure consistency across multiple operations?
Back: Transactions group operations into an atomic unit so all changes commit or all roll back, preventing partial updates.
Why: It matters because partial failure can corrupt related data.
Tradeoffs: Ensures correctness but adds overhead and locking.
Trap: Mistake: assuming individual queries are safe. Why wrong: partial success breaks invariants. Consequence: inconsistent state.
Scenario: Money transfer requires both debit and credit.

Front: What are the ACID properties and why do they matter in real systems?
Back: ACID guarantees atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability so transactions behave predictably even under failure.
Why: It matters because it prevents corruption and ensures recoverability.
When: Use ACID when correctness is critical.
Tradeoffs: Improves reliability but reduces performance and scalability.
Trap: Mistake: assuming ACID is free. Why wrong: requires coordination. Consequence: slower systems.
Scenario: Banking systems rely on ACID.

Front: What is isolation level and how does it impact concurrency?
Back: Isolation levels control visibility of changes between transactions, trading off consistency for concurrency.
Why: It matters because weaker levels allow anomalies while stronger levels reduce throughput.
When: Choose level based on consistency needs.
Tradeoffs: Higher isolation improves correctness but increases contention.
Trap: Mistake: always using highest isolation. Why wrong: unnecessary locking. Consequence: reduced throughput.
Scenario: Reporting can use lower isolation.

Front: What is a dirty read and why is it dangerous?
Back: A dirty read occurs when a transaction reads uncommitted data that may later be rolled back, making the read invalid.
Why: It matters because decisions may be based on data that never actually existed.
When: Avoid dirty reads in critical systems.
Tradeoffs: Improves concurrency but risks incorrect results.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring isolation. Why wrong: invalid reads occur. Consequence: incorrect logic.
Scenario: Report reads rolled-back data.

Front: What is normalization and why is it important?
Back: Normalization organizes data to eliminate redundancy so each fact is stored once, preventing inconsistent updates.
Why: It matters because duplicated data leads to anomalies and integrity issues.
Tradeoffs: Improves integrity but increases joins.
Trap: Mistake: over-normalizing blindly. Why wrong: excessive joins. Consequence: slow queries.
Scenario: Separating user and address avoids duplication.

Front: What problem does first normal form (1NF) solve?
Back: 1NF enforces atomic column values and removes repeating groups, making data queryable and consistent.
Why: It matters because non-atomic data breaks relational operations.
Tradeoffs: Improves structure but increases table complexity.
Trap: Mistake: storing arrays in columns. Why wrong: violates atomicity. Consequence: complex queries.
Scenario: Multiple phone numbers in one column break 1NF.

Front: What problem does third normal form (3NF) solve?
Back: 3NF removes transitive dependencies so non-key attributes depend only on the primary key, preventing duplicated derived data.
Why: It matters because redundancy leads to inconsistent updates.
Tradeoffs: Improves integrity but increases joins.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring dependencies. Why wrong: duplicates data. Consequence: inconsistent updates.
Scenario: Customer city stored in multiple tables.

Front: When should you denormalize a database schema?
Back: Denormalization duplicates data to reduce joins and improve read performance when query cost outweighs consistency concerns.
Why: It matters because joins can become bottlenecks at scale.
Tradeoffs: Improves reads but risks stale data.
Trap: Mistake: denormalizing too early. Why wrong: adds complexity. Consequence: maintenance issues.
Scenario: Precomputed aggregates reduce join cost.

Front: What are update anomalies and how does normalization prevent them?
Back: Update anomalies occur when redundant data must be updated in multiple places, risking inconsistencies, which normalization avoids by centralizing data.
Why: It matters because inconsistencies corrupt data integrity.
Tradeoffs: Improves integrity but may reduce performance.
Trap: Mistake: duplicating data. Why wrong: inconsistency risk. Consequence: conflicting values.
Scenario: Updating address in multiple rows causes mismatch.

Front: What is a database schema and why does it matter?
Back: A schema defines structure, relationships, and constraints, enforcing how data is stored and validated.
Why: It matters because it ensures consistency and correctness.
Tradeoffs: Improves integrity but reduces flexibility.
Trap: Mistake: treating schema casually. Why wrong: changes are costly. Consequence: complex migrations.
Scenario: Well-defined schema prevents invalid data.

Front: What is a primary key and why is it critical?
Back: A primary key uniquely identifies each row, enabling efficient lookups and enforcing uniqueness.
Why: It matters because identity is fundamental to relationships.
Tradeoffs: Improves integrity but requires careful design.
Trap: Mistake: using non-unique keys. Why wrong: breaks identity. Consequence: duplicates.
Scenario: User ID uniquely identifies users.

Front: What is a foreign key and what problem does it solve?
Back: A foreign key enforces relationships by ensuring referenced data exists, preventing orphan records.
Why: It matters because invalid references break data integrity.
Tradeoffs: Improves correctness but adds overhead.
Trap: Mistake: not using foreign keys. Why wrong: allows invalid data. Consequence: orphan records.
Scenario: Orders referencing missing users.

Front: What is referential integrity and why is it important?
Back: Referential integrity ensures relationships remain valid across tables, preventing broken links.
Why: It matters because invalid relationships corrupt queries.
Tradeoffs: Improves correctness but adds overhead.
Trap: Mistake: disabling constraints. Why wrong: invalid data. Consequence: broken joins.
Scenario: Deleting user leaves orphan orders.

Front: What is a composite key and when should you use it?
Back: A composite key combines multiple columns to uniquely identify a row when no single column suffices.
Why: It matters because it models real-world uniqueness.
When: Use when natural uniqueness spans columns.
Tradeoffs: Improves modeling but complicates queries.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring natural keys. Why wrong: duplicates allowed. Consequence: inconsistent data.
Scenario: Order items use order_id + product_id.

Front: What is database denormalization in caching strategies?
Back: Denormalization stores computed or duplicated data to avoid repeated joins and calculations, improving read performance.
Why: It matters because recomputation can be expensive.
Tradeoffs: Improves speed but risks stale data.
Trap: Mistake: not syncing duplicates. Why wrong: stale data. Consequence: incorrect results.
Scenario: Precomputed totals avoid recalculation.

Front: What is a deadlock in database transactions?
Back: A deadlock occurs when transactions hold locks that each other need, creating a cycle where none can proceed until the database aborts one to break the stalemate.
Why: It matters because deadlocks stop progress and force rollbacks, impacting reliability and throughput.
When: Use consistent lock ordering and short transactions to reduce risk.
Tradeoffs: Strict ordering reduces deadlocks but constrains design flexibility.
Trap: Mistake: acquiring locks in inconsistent order. Why wrong: creates circular wait. Consequence: transaction aborts and retries.
Scenario: Two transactions update rows in opposite order and block each other.

Front: What is write skew anomaly?
Back: Write skew happens when concurrent transactions read the same data and update different rows, violating constraints because each sees a valid snapshot.
Why: It matters because it breaks business rules without obvious conflicts.
When: Use higher isolation like serializable to prevent it.
Tradeoffs: Stronger isolation prevents anomalies but reduces concurrency.
Trap: Mistake: assuming default isolation prevents all anomalies. Why wrong: write skew is still possible. Consequence: invalid state.
Scenario: Two doctors both schedule shifts, exceeding allowed coverage.

Front: What is database sharding and how does it relate to schema design?
Back: Sharding partitions data across nodes using a shard key so each node handles a subset, reducing load on any single database.
Why: It matters because large datasets outgrow single-node capacity.
When: Use when vertical scaling is insufficient.
Tradeoffs: Improves scalability but complicates joins and rebalancing.
Trap: Mistake: choosing a poor shard key. Why wrong: uneven distribution. Consequence: hotspots and imbalance.
Scenario: User data sharded by region can overload one region.

Front: What is schema migration and why is it difficult?
Back: Schema migration changes database structure while preserving data and compatibility, requiring careful coordination between application and database.
Why: It matters because incorrect migrations can break running systems or corrupt data.
Tradeoffs: Enables evolution but introduces deployment risk.
Trap: Mistake: making breaking changes directly. Why wrong: active code depends on old schema. Consequence: downtime.
Scenario: Adding a non-null column without default breaks inserts.

Front: What is a materialized view and when should you use it?
Back: A materialized view stores precomputed query results so expensive aggregations do not need to be recomputed each time.
Why: It matters because repeated heavy queries can overwhelm the system.
Tradeoffs: Improves read speed but requires refresh logic.
Trap: Mistake: not refreshing view. Why wrong: data becomes stale. Consequence: incorrect results.
Scenario: Daily sales summary stored and refreshed periodically.

Front: What is a phantom read and when does it occur?
Back: A phantom read occurs when a transaction reruns a query and sees new rows inserted by another transaction, breaking the assumption of a stable dataset.
Why: It matters because repeated queries can yield inconsistent results.
When: Use serializable isolation to prevent it.
Tradeoffs: Higher isolation prevents anomalies but reduces concurrency.
Trap: Mistake: assuming repeatable read prevents all changes. Why wrong: new rows can still appear. Consequence: inconsistent results.
Scenario: Row count changes within a transaction.

Front: What is a savepoint in a transaction?
Back: A savepoint marks a point inside a transaction that you can roll back to without aborting the entire transaction.
Why: It matters because it allows partial recovery instead of losing all work.
When: Use when multiple steps can fail independently.
Tradeoffs: Improves flexibility but adds complexity.
Trap: Mistake: always rolling back entire transaction. Why wrong: discards valid work. Consequence: wasted processing.
Scenario: Batch insert rolls back only failed records.

Front: What is optimistic locking and when is it appropriate?
Back: Optimistic locking avoids holding locks and instead checks a version before commit to detect conflicts.
Why: It matters because it improves concurrency when conflicts are rare.
Tradeoffs: Improves throughput but requires retry handling.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring retries. Why wrong: conflicts overwrite data. Consequence: lost updates.
Scenario: Two users edit the same record simultaneously.

Front: What is pessimistic locking and when should you use it?
Back: Pessimistic locking locks data before modification to prevent conflicts, ensuring exclusive access.
Why: It matters because it guarantees correctness under contention.
Tradeoffs: Prevents conflicts but reduces concurrency.
Trap: Mistake: overusing locks. Why wrong: blocks others unnecessarily. Consequence: throughput drops.
Scenario: Inventory updates in high-demand systems.

Front: What is a database constraint and why is it important?
Back: Constraints enforce rules like uniqueness and referential integrity at the database level, ensuring invalid data cannot be stored.
Why: It matters because application-level checks can be bypassed or fail under concurrency.
When: Use constraints for critical business rules.
Tradeoffs: Improves integrity but reduces flexibility.
Trap: Mistake: relying only on application validation. Why wrong: race conditions occur. Consequence: invalid data.
Scenario: Unique constraint prevents duplicate emails.

Front: What is a check constraint and when should it be used?
Back: A check constraint enforces conditions on column values, ensuring only valid data is stored.
Why: It matters because it guarantees domain rules at the database level.
Tradeoffs: Improves correctness but adds overhead.
Trap: Mistake: skipping constraints. Why wrong: invalid values allowed. Consequence: broken logic.
Scenario: Age must be greater than zero.

Front: What is a surrogate key vs natural key?
Back: A surrogate key is an artificial identifier, while a natural key is derived from real-world data; surrogates remain stable while natural keys can change.
Why: It matters because key choice affects updates and relationships.
Tradeoffs: Surrogates simplify design but lose business meaning.
Trap: Mistake: using mutable natural keys. Why wrong: changes cascade. Consequence: complex updates.
Scenario: Email as key causes issues when changed.

Front: What is schema evolution and how do you manage it safely?
Back: Schema evolution updates structure over time while maintaining compatibility with existing data and applications.
Why: It matters because systems must evolve without breaking.
Tradeoffs: Safe changes reduce risk but increase complexity.
Trap: Mistake: making incompatible changes. Why wrong: breaks clients. Consequence: outages.
Scenario: Add nullable column before enforcing constraints.

Front: What is vertical vs horizontal partitioning?
Back: Vertical partitioning splits columns, horizontal partitioning splits rows, optimizing different access patterns.
Why: It matters because proper partitioning improves performance and scalability.
When: Choose based on query access patterns.
Tradeoffs: Improves efficiency but complicates queries.
Trap: Mistake: wrong partitioning choice. Why wrong: mismatched access. Consequence: slow queries.
Scenario: Split users by region vs columns.

Front: What is database replication lag and why does it matter?
Back: Replication lag is the delay between primary and replica updates, causing replicas to serve stale data.
Why: It matters because stale reads can break user expectations.
When: Use replicas carefully for read scaling.
Tradeoffs: Improves scalability but reduces consistency.
Trap: Mistake: assuming immediate consistency. Why wrong: lag exists. Consequence: stale reads.
Scenario: User sees outdated profile after update.

Front: What is write amplification in storage engines?
Back: Write amplification occurs when a single logical write results in multiple physical writes due to indexing or compaction.
Why: It matters because it increases I/O and reduces performance.
Tradeoffs: Improves durability but increases overhead.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring storage behavior. Why wrong: hidden cost. Consequence: slower writes.
Scenario: LSM compaction increases writes.

Front: What is read amplification and how does it affect queries?
Back: Read amplification happens when retrieving data requires accessing multiple locations or files, increasing latency.
Why: It matters because inefficient reads slow queries significantly.
When: Use indexing and caching to reduce it.
Tradeoffs: Improves flexibility but increases read cost.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring layout. Why wrong: scattered reads. Consequence: slow queries.
Scenario: Fetching data from multiple disk locations.

Front: What is a heap table vs clustered index?
Back: Heap tables store rows unordered, while clustered indexes store rows in sorted order based on a key, improving range access.
Why: It matters because physical layout affects query performance.
When: Use clustered indexes for range queries.
Tradeoffs: Improves reads but slows writes.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring layout. Why wrong: inefficient access. Consequence: slow scans.
Scenario: Range queries benefit from clustering.

Front: What is MVCC and how does it improve concurrency?
Back: MVCC keeps multiple versions of rows so readers don’t block writers, enabling non-blocking reads.
Why: It matters because locking can severely limit concurrency.
Tradeoffs: Improves reads but increases storage usage.
Trap: Mistake: assuming no cost. Why wrong: version storage grows. Consequence: bloat.
Scenario: Postgres uses MVCC for concurrency.

Front: What is a redo log and why is it important?
Back: Redo logs record changes so they can be replayed after a crash, ensuring durability.
Why: It matters because without it, committed data could be lost.
Tradeoffs: Improves recovery but adds write overhead.
Trap: Mistake: disabling logs. Why wrong: no recovery. Consequence: data loss.
Scenario: Crash recovery replays redo logs.

Front: What is a write-ahead log (WAL)?
Back: WAL ensures changes are logged before being applied to disk, guaranteeing durability and recovery consistency.
Why: It matters because it prevents corruption during crashes.
Tradeoffs: Improves safety but adds latency.
Trap: Mistake: bypassing WAL. Why wrong: inconsistent state. Consequence: corruption.
Scenario: Postgres WAL ensures crash recovery.

Front: What is a covering index and why is it useful?
Back: A covering index includes all columns needed for a query so it can be served entirely from the index, avoiding table access.
Why: It matters because reducing I/O significantly improves performance.
Tradeoffs: Improves speed but increases index size.
Trap: Mistake: overusing covering indexes. Why wrong: bloats index. Consequence: slower writes.
Scenario: Index contains all needed columns.

Front: What is cardinality and why does it matter?
Back: Cardinality measures how unique values are in a column, influencing how effective an index is.
Why: It matters because low-cardinality indexes are often ignored.
Tradeoffs: Improves selectivity but not always performance.
Trap: Mistake: indexing low-cardinality columns. Why wrong: low usefulness. Consequence: wasted index.
Scenario: Boolean column rarely benefits.

Front: What is a hot partition and how does it affect scaling?
Back: A hot partition occurs when one shard receives disproportionate traffic, becoming a bottleneck.
Why: It matters because uneven load limits scalability.
Tradeoffs: Improves distribution but requires planning.
Trap: Mistake: poor shard key. Why wrong: uneven load. Consequence: bottleneck.
Scenario: User ID hashing distributes load evenly.

Front: What is eventual consistency in database systems?
Back: Eventual consistency allows temporary inconsistencies between replicas, with data converging over time.
Why: It matters because it enables high availability and scalability.
When: Use in distributed systems where stale reads are acceptable.
Tradeoffs: Improves availability but risks stale data.
Trap: Mistake: assuming immediate consistency. Why wrong: delays exist. Consequence: incorrect reads.
Scenario: Social feeds tolerate eventual consistency.

Front: What is strong consistency and when is it required?
Back: Strong consistency ensures all reads return the latest committed data, guaranteeing correctness.
Why: It matters because critical systems cannot tolerate stale data.
Tradeoffs: Improves correctness but reduces scalability.
Trap: Mistake: using everywhere. Why wrong: unnecessary cost. Consequence: slower systems.
Scenario: Bank balances require accuracy.

Front: What is a secondary index and how does it differ from primary index?
Back: A secondary index is an additional structure that provides alternate lookup paths, while the primary index defines row identity.
Why: It matters because queries often need access beyond primary keys.
Tradeoffs: Improves reads but increases write cost.
Trap: Mistake: too many indexes. Why wrong: overhead. Consequence: slow writes.
Scenario: Email index for user lookup.

Front: What is data locality and why does it matter?
Back: Data locality keeps related data physically close, reducing latency and improving performance.
Why: It matters because remote access is slower.
Tradeoffs: Improves performance but requires planning.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring locality. Why wrong: remote cost. Consequence: slow queries.
Scenario: Store user data near region.

Front: What is a tombstone in database systems?
Back: A tombstone marks deleted data for later cleanup instead of immediate removal.
Why: It matters because it supports eventual consistency and replication.
Tradeoffs: Simplifies deletes but increases storage.
Trap: Mistake: not cleaning up tombstones. Why wrong: accumulation. Consequence: degraded performance.
Scenario: Cassandra uses tombstones for deletes.

Front: What is compaction in LSM-based databases?
Back: Compaction merges data files to remove duplicates and stale entries, keeping storage efficient.
Why: It matters because without it, reads become inefficient.
Tradeoffs: Improves reads but consumes resources.
Trap: Mistake: ignoring compaction. Why wrong: buildup occurs. Consequence: latency spikes.
Scenario: Cassandra compacts SSTables in background.
